fragments.content: Dementia is a syndrome of progressive cognitive decline interfering with daily functioning, affecting ~10% of those >65 years, ~30% >85 years. Major types: Alzheimer's disease (60-80%, memory-predominant early, amyloid/tau pathology), vascular dementia (20%, stepwise decline, executive dysfunction prominent, cerebrovascular disease on imaging), Lewy body dementia (LBD, 5-10%, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuating cognition, REM sleep behavior disorder, alpha-synuclein pathology), frontotemporal dementia (FTD, 5-10%, behavioral variant—disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy; or primary progressive aphasia—language predominant; tau or TDP-43 pathology). Mixed dementia (AD + vascular) common in elderly. Reversible causes ('treatable dementias'): normal pressure hydrocephalus, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, depression ('pseudodementia'), medication effects (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines), sleep apnea, neurosyphilis, HIV.

Dementia treatment includes cognitive enhancers, non-pharmacologic interventions, and management of behavioral symptoms. Alzheimer's/Lewy body/Parkinson's disease dementia: cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) provide modest symptomatic benefit for cognition/function; rivastigmine patch reduces GI side effects. Memantine (NMDA antagonist) for moderate-severe AD, can combine with ChEI. Vascular dementia: focus on vascular risk reduction (BP control, statins, diabetes management, antiplatelets); ChEI may provide modest benefit. FTD: no effective cognitive enhancers; ChEI may worsen behavior. Non-pharmacologic: cognitive stimulation, structured routines, caregiver education/support, environmental modifications (safety, reduce overstimulation), advance care planning. Behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD): first-line non-drug approaches (identify triggers, modify environment, music therapy, validation therapy). Pharmacologic only for severe distress/danger: antidepressants (SSRIs for depression/anxiety), antipsychotics (atypical—quetiapine, risperidone) only for severe agitation/psychosis unresponsive to non-drug measures (black box warning: increased mortality in dementia patients; use lowest dose, shortest duration, informed consent). Avoid benzodiazepines (falls, confusion, paradoxical agitation).

Dementia diagnosis uses DSM-5 criteria for major neurocognitive disorder: significant cognitive decline from prior level in ≥1 domain (complex attention, executive function, learning/memory, language, perceptual-motor, social cognition) interfering with independence; not occurring exclusively during delirium; not better explained by another mental disorder. Evaluation: (1) History from patient and informant (onset, progression, functional impact). (2) Cognitive screening: MMSE (copyrighted), MoCA (more sensitive for MCI, scores <26 abnormal), Mini-Cog (3-word recall + clock draw). Formal neuropsychological testing characterizes pattern (hippocampal in AD, frontal-subcortical in vascular/FTD). (3) Laboratory: CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, folate; consider RPR, HIV if risk factors. (4) Imaging: MRI brain (preferred) or CT to assess atrophy pattern, vascular burden, exclude reversible causes (tumor, SDH, NPH). AD shows medial temporal/hippocampal atrophy; vascular shows infarcts, white matter disease; FTD shows frontal/anterior temporal atrophy; LBD may show occipital hypometabolism on FDG-PET. DaT scan (dopamine transporter SPECT) supports LBD diagnosis (reduced striatal uptake). Differential: delirium (acute onset, inattention, fluctuating course), depression (prominent mood symptoms, effort-dependent cognitive testing).
